paper.title: Creative foraging: An experimental paradigm for studying exploration and discovery

arc_summary: The introduction is structured as a quick M1 legitimation followed by a layered M2 critique that **interlaces two brief M1 interludes** ([8], [14]) — each reporting a prior finding immediately before it is critiqued — creating a tight gap-by-example rhythm. **M3 is long and internally complex**: it opens with a dual announcement ([16]–[17]), grounds the borrowed foraging paradigm with cited prior work ([18]–[21]), then alternates between design-justification (3B) and formal hypothesis statements (3C), including an **in-line contrast against naïve OFT** ([35]–[39]) that justifies H3 without reopening the paper's gap. The arc closes cleanly with a forward-pointer to Results ([47]).

move_roles.role: Defines creative exploration and asserts its centrality across art, design, science, and cognitive development, establishing the topic as broadly significant before the gap argument begins.

Opens the gap argument with a broad knowledge-limitation claim, then narrows it to the specific methodological obstacle of non-automated, hard-to-quantify paradigms, using the AUT as a concrete illustration.
Briefly reports a specific empirical finding from AUT research (category-switching) to set up the immediately following critique of what the AUT fails to capture.
Resumes the critique by targeting the AUT's manual coding burden, its undefined solution space, and — most fundamentally — its blindness to the intermediate steps of the search process.
Reports the think-aloud method as a prior attempt to address process-invisibility, positioning it as the next target for critique rather than as genuine prior art establishing the field.
Closes the gap-establishment block by dismissing think-aloud methods as difficult to code and quantify, completing the cumulative case that no existing paradigm solves the measurement problem.

Announces the creative foraging paradigm, grounds it in prior human-foraging work, describes the game mechanics, justifies the design's analytic affordances, formally states five hypotheses (H1–H5) with in-line theoretical justification for H3 and H4, and closes with a forward-pointer to the Results section.

sentences.text: Creative exploration and discovery processes are defined as the search for novel and valuable elements within a set of constraints.
sentences.note: Introduces the paper's working definition of the core construct.

sentences.text: Creative exploration is central for many fields, including art [1], design [2] and science [3].
sentences.note: Asserts the significance/relevance of the topic across domains.

sentences.text: More generally, it is an integral part of cognitive development [4,5].
sentences.note: Extends the importance claim to a further domain.

sentences.text: Despite progress achieved in understanding creative search processes from different perspectives [6–14], much is still unknown about their underlying dynamics and mechanisms.
sentences.move_note: Concessive 'despite progress' clause nods back to M1 territory, but the rhetorical center is the gap claim, so coded 2B.

sentences.text: One obstacle for understanding creative exploration is the lack of automated and high-resolution experimental paradigms.
sentences.note: Identifies a specific methodological gap underlying [4].
sentences.move_note: Narrows the gap named in [4] to a specific methodological obstacle.

sentences.text: For example, one of the most commonly used tests for creativity is the alternate usage task (AUT) [15].
sentences.note: Gives a concrete example of the paradigm-gap claim in [5]. Parent: [5] (nostat).

sentences.text: In the AUT participants are asked to come up with creative uses for common objects (e.g., a ping-pong ball).
sentences.note: Unpacks how the AUT works; re-parented to [5] (nostat), since original parent [6] is secondary.

sentences.text: Participants show exploration patterns such as switching between categories of solutions [12].
sentences.note: Reports a specific empirical finding from prior work, with citation.
sentences.move_note: Brief M1 interlude: reports a specific empirical finding from AUT research (subject = participants/study), embedded within the surrounding M2 critique block. Locally interlaced M1/M2.

sentences.text: This test is coded manually, a laborious process which limits the extent of feasible studies.
sentences.note: Introduces a limitation of the AUT.
sentences.move_note: Coded 2B (naming what is lacking/limited) rather than 2A, since this critiques a method's adequacy rather than directly challenging a specific prior claim.

sentences.text: The test is difficult to analyze and model because the space of possible solutions is undefined (how many different usages for a ping-pong ball exist?) and has no natural notion of similarity between different solutions.
sentences.note: Unpacks/elaborates the limitation named in [9]. Parent: [9] (contrast).

sentences.text: Other common creativity tests share these limitations [16].
sentences.note: Generalizes the critique from AUT specifically to creativity tests broadly, with citation support.
sentences.move_note: Generalizes the AUT-specific critique to creativity tests broadly; continues 2B.

sentences.text: More fundamentally, AUT and other common creativity tests record only the discovered solutions and not the intermediate steps leading from one solution to another.
sentences.note: Opens a new, deeper line of critique.
sentences.move_note: Opens a deeper, more fundamental gap claim (loss of process information); continues 2B.

sentences.text: Thus, they do not allow insight into the process of exploration.
sentences.note: Draws out the implication of [12]'s claim. Parent: [12] (contrast).

sentences.text: Several studies attempted to map the processes leading to creative solutions by recording verbal transcripts of thought processes—the 'think aloud' method [2,17].
sentences.note: Reports a specific prior research approach with citation.
sentences.move_note: Brief M1 interlude reporting what prior studies did (subject = the studies), parallel in function to [8]. Locally interlaced M1/M2.

sentences.text: However, these methods are difficult to code and quantitatively analyze.
sentences.note: Limitation of the think-aloud method just introduced in [14].
sentences.move_note: Closes out the think-aloud critique and the gap-establishing block, returning to 2B after the M1 interlude in [14].

sentences.text: Here, we introduce a new paradigm for creative exploration that allows automated tracking of the search process in a space of possibilities that is large yet well-defined.
sentences.note: Explicit novelty claim marking the transition to the present work.
sentences.move_note: Explicit novstat transition; opens the M3 block that runs to the end of the introduction.

sentences.text: To achieve this, we borrow techniques from the study of human foraging.
sentences.note: States the methodological approach/strategy of the current paper.
sentences.move_note: Continues the purpose statement begun in [16], specifying the methodological strategy.

sentences.text: In a series of studies Hills, Todd and colleagues used a spatial foraging task and a letter puzzle task to experimentally study human foraging behavior [18–21].
sentences.note: Reports prior research findings/methods with citation.
sentences.move_note: Tempstat used as context-setting within M3 (per decision rules) to ground the borrowed foraging paradigm; coded 3B rather than M1/1C because its function here is to justify the present design choice, not to build the paper's own gap argument.

sentences.text: These experimental foraging tasks employed solution spaces which are finite and well-defined [22].
sentences.note: Elaborates a property of the tasks introduced in [18]. Parent: [18] (tempstat).

sentences.text: For example, in the spatial foraging task participants searched for resources on a grid of locations.
sentences.note: Concrete example of the foraging task type; re-parented to [18] (tempstat), since original parent [19] is secondary.

sentences.text: This task records intermediate steps between solutions and has a clear distance metric, features that allow the foraging behavior to be quantitatively analyzed and mathematically modeled using optimal foraging theory (OFT) [23].
sentences.note: Reports a substantive claim about the paradigm's analytic value, with citation to OFT.
sentences.move_note: Continues justifying the borrowed paradigm by naming the analytic properties that motivate adopting it for the present design.

sentences.text: In order to develop a similar task for creative search, we need to address one key difference between foraging and creative search.
sentences.note: States the paper's goal/rationale for adapting the foraging paradigm.
sentences.move_note: States a design challenge to resolve when adapting foraging methods; coded 3B (design rationale) rather than a return to M2, since the paper's gap was already established and this elaborates how the present design must address it.

sentences.text: In the foraging tasks, the goal is to find as many solutions as possible; in creativity tests the goal is two-fold: (i) to find many solutions (fluency) (ii) of high quality (novel and valuable).
sentences.note: Unpacks the "key difference" flagged in [22]. Parent: [22] (contribution).

sentences.text: In other words, whereas the value of the different solutions in the foraging tasks is constant (for example, all foraged pixels in the spatial foraging task are equally valuable), the distribution of solution values in creativity tests is skewed, with many low-quality solutions (boring or non-feasible) and only a handful of high-quality creative solutions.
sentences.note: Further unpacks the key difference; re-parented to [22] (contribution), since original parent [23] is secondary.

sentences.text: We present a paradigm for creative foraging in which players seek solutions that they find interesting and beautiful, embedded within a well-defined search space.
sentences.note: Announces the paper's specific paradigm/contribution.
sentences.move_note: Core announcement of the paradigm; re-asserts 3A after the justification detour in [18]-[24].

sentences.text: The creative foraging game enables high-resolution measurement of creative, intrinsically motivated search in the framework of visual exploration and discovery [24–28].
sentences.note: Further specifies the purpose/framing of the contribution.
sentences.move_note: States what the design 'enables', matching 3B's defining template ('this design allows us to...').

sentences.text: The creative foraging game is a computer game in which players can move ten adjacent squares to create different shapes (Fig 1).
sentences.note: Describes the method/task structure of the current study.
sentences.move_note: Method/task description continuing the purpose announcement.

sentences.text: There are 36,446 possible shapes (Section A in S1 File).
sentences.note: Elaborates a specific parameter of the task described in [27]. Parent: [27] (contribution).

sentences.text: The players are instructed to save shapes that they find interesting and beautiful into a gallery.
sentences.note: Continues describing task mechanics; re-parented to [27] (contribution), since original parent [28] is secondary.

sentences.text: The timing of all moves and gallery choices is recorded.
sentences.note: Continues task description; re-parented to [27] (contribution), since original parent [29] is secondary.

sentences.text: This setting allows us to observe all intermediate steps as people search a large but finite metric space of different possibilities.
sentences.note: Synthesizes the preceding task-description sentences ([27]–[30]). Parent: [27] (contribution).
sentences.move_note: Although parented to [27] (3A), the content explicitly states what the design 'allows' -- matches 3B's defining template more precisely than 3A, so overridden accordingly.

sentences.text: We present data on 100 participants and interpret the results in the context of OFT according to the following hypotheses:
sentences.note: Announces scope (sample) and structure (hypotheses to follow) of the current paper.
sentences.move_note: Bridges the design description to the hypothesis list by describing study scope and analytic framework; coded 3A (continuing 'what we did'), distinct from the hypotheses themselves coded 3C below.

sentences.text: (H1) Participants' trajectories in shape space are composed of distinguishable phases of exploration and exploitation: exploration for new shape categories and exploitation of similar shapes within a category.
sentences.note: States a specific hypothesis of the current study.
sentences.move_note: First formally stated hypothesis; pre-announces an expected pattern of results, matching 3C.

sentences.text: (H2) In phases of exploitation people move along optimal (shortest) paths.
sentences.note: States a specific hypothesis of the current study.
sentences.move_note: Second formal hypothesis; same step as [33].

sentences.text: Our third hypothesis diverges from the predictions of a naïve version of OFT, in which there is a constant value for any shape collected.
sentences.note: Frames H3 as diverging from a prior theoretical account (naïve OFT).
sentences.move_note: Locally contrasts H3 against naive OFT predictions to justify the hypothesis; coded 3B (in-line justification) rather than a return to M2, since this challenges a theoretical baseline internal to the paper's framework, not the paper's own gap. Not a true M2-M3 cycle.

sentences.text: The performance metric in naïve OFT is therefore the amount of collected resources.
sentences.note: Unpacks the naïve OFT account referenced in [35]. Parent: [35] (contrast).

sentences.text: Hence naïve OFT predicts that exploitation of a given patch is terminated due to diminishing returns caused by depletion of the patch.
sentences.note: Continues unpacking the naïve OFT prediction; re-parented to [35] (contrast), since original parent [36] is secondary.

sentences.text: In contrast, in creative foraging the intrinsic interest 'value' of collected resources is important.
sentences.note: Explicitly opposes the naïve OFT account elaborated in [35]–[37].
sentences.move_note: Continues the naive-OFT contrast as justification for H3; same reasoning as [35].

sentences.text: Collection from a given patch may lead to diminishing interest, far before the patch is depleted.
sentences.note: Extends the contrast claim in [38] into an unevidenced prediction. Parent: [38] (contrast).

sentences.text: We therefore hypothesize that: (H3) In creative foraging participants leave exploitation phases sooner than predicted by naïve OFT, far before patch depletion.
sentences.note: States H3 formally.
sentences.move_note: Formal statement of H3; matches 3C, same step as [33]-[34].

sentences.text: The next hypothesis concerns the moment when a new category of shapes is discovered.
sentences.note: Pure structural announcement of the upcoming topic (H4); makes no independent claim. Parent: [40] (contribution).
sentences.move_note: Purely structural/transitional sentence previewing H4, with no independent claim; inherits parent [40]'s step (3C) by default rather than being treated as a distinct article-structure (3D) statement, since it previews a single hypothesis rather than the article as a whole.

sentences.text: We suggest that entry points to phases of exploitation can serve as an experimental proxy for creative leaps.
sentences.note: States the paper's proposed conceptual move/claim.
sentences.move_note: States the conceptual claim motivating H4; coded 3B (justification) preceding the formal hypothesis in [45].

sentences.text: Qualitative descriptions of creative processes suggest that creative leaps often occur in the periphery of a field [29], through a road that is not often taken.
sentences.note: Reports a finding/characterization from prior cited work.
sentences.move_note: Reviews prior qualitative findings to justify H4; tempstat-as-context-setting within M3, paralleling [18]-[21].

sentences.text: In addition, creativity was previously linked to ambiguity [30].
sentences.note: Reports another prior finding with citation.
sentences.move_note: Reviews another prior finding to justify H4; same function as [43].

sentences.text: We therefore hypothesize that: (H4) Shapes at the entry points to phases of exploitation are more ambiguous, in the sense that they can lead different players to different categories.
sentences.note: States H4 formally.
sentences.move_note: Formal statement of H4; matches 3C.

sentences.text: Finally, we hypothesize that (H5) People show individual differences in their search strategies and patterns.
sentences.note: States H5 formally.
sentences.move_note: Formal statement of H5; matches 3C, closing the hypothesis list.

sentences.text: In the results section we operationalize these hypotheses in terms of the creative foraging task.
sentences.note: Announces the structure/plan of the paper going forward.
sentences.move_note: Explicit forward-pointer to the Results section; classic terminal 3D, closing the introduction.
